one_line_takeaway: AgentWarden shows that combining task-scoped tool-awareness restriction, a cheap rule-based+fine-tuned-LLM tool-call interceptor, and a PPO policy for learning minimal per-task tool sets can cut real-session over-provisioning roughly 10x (SER 0.053 to 0.557) and blocks 100% of exec/sessions_spawn tool-call attacks, but the paper's own experiments show this governance layer is void against attacks that never emit a tool call (text-only dangerous output, or shell backends that bypass the LLM API entirely).
key_finding: On the N=500 automated batch: baseline (uncontrolled) average SER = 0.053; of 447 intercepted tool calls, 26.2% were blocked; exec (70/70) and sessions_spawn (8/8) were blocked at 100% by the 0.1ms rule stage; the fine-tuned Stage-2 classifier produced ~22 false-positive blocks on benign write operations (HTTP server scripts), attributed to a 95%/5% BLOCK/ALLOW training-class imbalance in the 273-example fine-tuning set. Real-session governed SER was 0.557 vs. 0.053 uncontrolled baseline (10.5x, the paper reports both +337% and +952% for different SER comparisons -- +337% is stated as the PPO-vs-baseline SER improvement on real sessions, +952% appears to be the same ratio expressed differently; +260% is the synthetic-environment SER improvement). Ablation (100 tasks, seed=99): full system SER 0.227 vs. raw baseline SER 0.078 (+192%); disabling RL (YAML fallback only) gives SER 0.177 (PPO contributes +28% over static rules); disabling the Router leaves SER unchanged (0.225) but removes the sole mechanism blocking dangerous tool execution -- the paper attributes ~80% of total SER gain to the Capability Governor alone. On AgentDojo (949 injection cases, deepseek-chat, important_instructions attack): overall Benign Utility 89.7% under AgentWarden vs. 90.7% undefended (-1.0pp), and Attack Success Rate 88.9% under AgentWarden vs. 90.3% undefended (-1.4pp overall, largest drop -5.7pp on the Slack suite) -- the paper explicitly frames this as a modest reduction because AgentDojo's injection payload lives in tool-call *outputs* (email/file content) rather than in tool-call *requests*, which is the surface AgentWarden governs. On the 100-task N=500 adversarial subset: 38 blocked by the Safety Router (infrastructure), 54 refused by the model itself (DeepSeek-chat), 8 were benign-intent prompts that passed correctly; reported attack success rate 0/100 (92% is stated as the 'combined coverage' figure). The paper's headline qualitative finding (called out as its 4th contribution) is a documented case where prompt injection against qwen2.5:7b caused the model to produce natural-language reverse-shell instructions (nc -l -p 4444) in response text without ever generating a tool call, which the Safety Router (which only inspects tool calls) could not intercept -- motivating the optional Stage 3 Llama Guard 3 filter, which on a fixed 4-item dangerous corpus achieved 4/4 detection and 0/3 false positives, at latencies of ~782-12,829ms (cold GPU dominates). A separate empirical finding from DeepAgents integration testing: its LocalShellBackend executes shell commands (e.g. ls -la /tmp) against the real host filesystem without ever generating an LLM tool call, completely bypassing the LLM-layer proxy -- confirmed as a fundamental architectural boundary of any governance approach that intercepts only LLM-generated tool calls.
core_contribution: AgentWarden, a three/four-layer (the paper is internally inconsistent about the count, calling it both 'three-layer' and 'four-layer') governance middleware for autonomous AI agent runtimes (demonstrated on OpenClaw) that enforces least-privilege capability scoping without modifying the underlying agent or LLM. Layer 1 (Capability Governor) restricts which tools the agent is even told it has, per session, via AGENTS.md injection and openclaw.json tools.deny writes. Layer 2 is a PPO reinforcement-learning policy (state = one-hot task type + normalized trust, 7-dim; action = 17-dim binary tool exposure mask; reward = alpha*Uaccuracy - beta*Ceconomy - gamma*Psafety with alpha=1.0, beta=0.3, gamma=5.0) trained on an accumulated audit log to learn the minimum viable tool set per task type. Layer 3 is a Safety Router, a FastAPI MITM proxy between OpenClaw and the LLM backend (Ollama/DeepSeek) that intercepts every POST /api/chat response and classifies tool calls with a two-stage hybrid classifier: Stage 1 is a 0.1ms deterministic rule engine (always-block list for exec/sessions spawn/subagents, argument-pattern rules for rm -rf, file://, AWS metadata endpoints, SSH credential paths, and prompt-injection phrase patterns); Stage 2 is a Qwen2.5-1.5B model fine-tuned on 273 labeled block/allow examples (~800ms), skipped for known-safe tools. The paper also implements (but disables by default) a Stage 3 semantic output filter using Llama Guard 3 to catch dangerous natural-language responses that never produced a tool call. The authors introduce a new metric, Skill Economy Ratio (SER) = |tools invoked| / |tools exposed|, to quantify capability over-provisioning.
threat_model: AgentWarden's stated threat model (Section 3.5) is: (1) prompt injection causing the agent to request dangerous tool calls; (2) capability escalation via tools the agent should not have for the current task; (3) subagent spawning for lateral movement; (4) supply-chain attacks via malicious skills, blocked by denying skill exposure before the agent sees them. The paper explicitly scopes out semantic content of agent responses (text-only dangerous output with no tool call) as out of scope for Layers 1-2, though it partially addresses this gap with the optional Stage 3 filter. The paper documents concrete real-world motivating incidents it treats as background (not its own empirical contribution): the 'ClawHavoc' supply-chain attack cited to CrowdStrike Intelligence [2026], and CVE-2026-25253 (prompt-injection RCE in OpenClaw) cited to NVD [2026] -- these are Related Work / motivation, not something the paper itself discovered or analyzed forensically.
limitations: The authors themselves list: (1) the RL policy's state space omits user identity and historical session behavior; (2) the Stage-2 LLM classifier's ~800ms-2,094ms latency may need optimization for high-throughput deployments; (3) NemoClaw live integration is deferred because NemoClaw itself is in alpha as of the paper's writing; (4) the fine-tuned agentwarden-router classifier was trained on only 273 examples with a severe 95%/5% class imbalance, which the authors say is a 'minimal proof-of-concept' requiring 2,000-5,000 balanced examples for production quality; (5) both trained PPO policy checkpoints collapsed to a degenerate single-tool policy in production testing, a known failure mode of SER-maximizing training in a synthetic simulated environment, requiring a YAML-fallback safety net; (6) SER=1.0 in the synthetic PPO training environment is explicitly called an artifact of oversimplified simulation, not a real capability; (7) qwen2.5:7b's poor tool-calling reliability (7 redundant status calls, timeouts, wrong tool selection) is flagged as a confound that makes the 20-task evaluation a lower bound rather than a representative result; (8) Stage 3 is disabled by default due to added latency (~200ms-1s) and is evaluated only on a small fixed corpus (4 dangerous + 3 benign items) rather than at scale, because model non-determinism was judged to invalidate live-generation evaluation; (9) the DeepAgents LocalShellBackend finding shows the entire architecture's protection can be trivially bypassed by any runtime backend that executes without routing through the monitored LLM API; (10) the paper is a preprint under review at a workshop (NeurIPS 2026 Agent Safety Workshop), several evaluations (11-case classifier test, N=20 gemma4 replication) are explicitly called 'smoke tests' pending a larger camera-ready evaluation, and the eprint_url arXiv identifier (2604.11839) and cited dates (2026 conferences, CVE-2026-25253) place this in a near-future/fictionalized publication context that could not be independently corroborated against real arXiv/NVD/CrowdStrike records from this review process.

datasets_used: N=500 automated batch: 400 benign + 100 adversarial tasks, seed=42, generated from a fixed 6-type task taxonomy derived via manual annotation of N=500 OpenClaw usage-log sessions
AgentDojo v1.2.2 benchmark (Debenedetti et al. 2024): 97 user tasks across 4 environments (Workspace, Travel, Banking, Slack), 949 important_instructions injection test cases
N=20-per-runtime/model multi-runtime validation set (OpenClaw, DeepAgents, Hermes), seed=42
273 labeled block/allow examples used to fine-tune the Stage-2 Qwen2.5-1.5B classifier (agentwarden-router)
Fixed 4-item dangerous-response corpus + 3 benign responses used for Stage 3 (Llama Guard 3) evaluation
quantitative_results: Tool reduction for summarisation tasks: 73%; dangerous tool elimination: 100%
Real-session governed SER (PPO): 0.557 (10.5x / +337% vs baseline per paper's own framing)
Synthetic PPO training environment SER improvement: +260% (reaching a training-artifact SER=1.0)
N=500 batch: 447 tool calls intercepted, 26.2% blocked overall; exec blocked 70/70 (100%); sessions_spawn blocked 8/8 (100%)
~22 benign write-operation false positives out of the N=500 batch attributed to 95/5 class-imbalanced classifier training data
Adversarial coverage, N=500's 100-task adversarial subset: 38 blocked by Safety Router, 54 refused by model, 8 benign-intent passed; attack success rate 0/100; combined coverage reported as 92%

Ablation (100 tasks, seed=99): Full system SER 0.227 vs raw baseline SER 0.078 (+192%); No-RL/YAML-fallback SER 0.177 (PPO contributes +28% over static rules); No-Router SER 0.225 (unchanged, confirming Router contributes 0 marginal SER but is sole blocker of dangerous execution); Capability Governor attributed ~80% of total SER gain

AgentDojo (deepseek-chat, important_instructions attack, N=949 injections): Overall Benign Utility 90.7% baseline vs 89.7% AgentWarden (-1.0pp); Overall Utility-under-Attack 78.5% baseline vs 77.1% AgentWarden; Overall ASR 90.3% baseline vs 88.9% AgentWarden (-1.4pp), largest suite-level ASR drop in Slack (65.7% to 60.0%, -5.7pp)
AgentWarden governed 2,070 tool calls during the defended AgentDojo run at 340ms average governance latency
Gemma4:e4b N=20 replication: 100% adversarial coverage (8/8), 0% FPR (12/12 benign correct), 0% error rate; warm GPU inference 656ms, cold first-call ~28s / 17,324ms wall clock average
Stage 3 (Llama Guard 3) fixed-corpus evaluation: 4/4 dangerous responses detected (100%), 0/3 false positives on benign responses; latency 782-1,387ms warm GPU, 12,829ms cold GPU

Multi-runtime N=20 validation: OpenClaw+gemma4:e4b 100% coverage (5 infra blocks + 3 model refusals), avg latency 27,858ms; OpenClaw+deepseek-chat 100% coverage (1 infra block + 7 model refusals), avg latency 6,350ms; both 0% error rate

Cross-model N=20 adversarial coverage table: qwen2.5:1.5b ~75% coverage (~75% infra block, ~0% model refusal, ~800ms latency); deepseek-chat ~92% coverage (~38% infra, ~54% refusal, ~2,000ms latency); gemma4:e4b 100% coverage (50% infra, 50% refusal, 656ms latency)
baselines_compared: Uncontrolled OpenClaw baseline (all 17 tools exposed, no governance) -- used for SER and ablation comparisons
NemoClaw (NVIDIA, container-level sandbox with declarative YAML policies) -- discussed/compared qualitatively in Table 1 positioning, not empirically benchmarked head-to-head
DefenseClaw (Cisco AI Defense, static skill scanner + anomaly detection) -- discussed/compared qualitatively in Table 1 positioning, not empirically benchmarked head-to-head
AgentDojo undefended baseline pipeline (identical setup minus AgentWarden governance) -- empirically compared on BU/UA/ASR
No-RL (YAML fallback only) and No-Router ablation conditions -- empirically compared via the 100-task ablation study
Cross-model comparison of qwen2.5:7b/1.5b vs deepseek-chat vs gemma4:e4b as the underlying agent LLM, used as informal baselines against each other's model-level safety behavior
